questions.prompt: A project costs $1,000 today and pays $500 at the end of each of the next three years. The cost of capital is 10%. What is the NPV? Answer in dollars to the nearest cent.
Two mutually exclusive projects: A has NPV = $100 and IRR = 30%; B has NPV = $1,000 and IRR = 15%. Cost of capital is 10%. Which should the firm take?
choices: A — higher IRR means better return on capital
B — NPV measures dollar value created and is the correct criterion for mutually exclusive projects
questions.explanation: IRR can favor smaller projects with higher percentage returns. NPV correctly compares dollar value created. For mutually exclusive choices, take the higher NPV.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are real problems with using payback period as a project-selection criterion?
choices: Ignores cash flows after the cutoff date
Can reject very long-lived high-NPV projects
questions.explanation: Payback ignores TVM and tail cash flows, leading to rejection of long-lived projects with large positive NPV. Being easy to compute is a feature, not a bug — just not enough to make it a good decision rule.

questions.prompt: When can IRR give multiple valid answers (so the IRR rule becomes ambiguous)?
choices: When the project has only positive cash flows
When the cash flow changes sign more than once (e.g., investment, profits, then a large closure cost)
When the discount rate is above 10%
Whenever the cash flows include depreciation
questions.explanation: Non-conventional cash-flow streams with multiple sign changes can have multiple IRRs. NPV is unambiguous in all cases.

furtherReading.title: NPV and Other Investment Criteria (Brealey lecture notes)
furtherReading.source: Pace University course notes (based on Brealey/Myers)
furtherReading.why: Free university lecture slides built on the canonical Brealey/Myers treatment that grounds when NPV and IRR agree and when they conflict.
